metadata.description: Comprehensive mental models repository for Quran Visual Cards

venn_diagram.name: Venn Diagram
venn_diagram.description: Two or more overlapping circles showing intersections and shared areas
venn_diagram.sketch_base: Two large hand-drawn overlapping circles with labeled regions and highlighted intersection zone

cycle_loop.name: Cycle Loop
cycle_loop.description: Circular flow with arrows showing recurring patterns and feedback mechanisms
cycle_loop.sketch_base: Hand-drawn circular path with thick curved arrows flowing continuously around, returning to start point

sequential_chain.name: Sequential Chain
sequential_chain.description: Linear progression showing step-by-step progression from one point to another
sequential_chain.sketch_base: Horizontal row of connected boxes with arrows moving left to right, each box slightly raised or lowered for visual interest

decision_tree.name: Decision Tree
decision_tree.description: Branching tree structure showing yes/no paths and multiple possible outcomes
decision_tree.sketch_base: Tree trunk splitting into branches with labels like 'yes' and 'no' at each branch point, leaves at endpoints representing outcomes

matrix_2x2.name: 2x2 Matrix
matrix_2x2.description: Four quadrants created by two intersecting axes with labels on each axis
matrix_2x2.sketch_base: Large square divided into four equal quadrants by perpendicular lines, axis labels on sides with subtle shading in different quadrants

pyramid_hierarchy.name: Pyramid Hierarchy
pyramid_hierarchy.description: Stacked triangular levels showing hierarchy, progression, or dependencies
pyramid_hierarchy.sketch_base: Hand-drawn pyramid with 4-5 horizontal sections stacked from wide base to narrow top, each section clearly separated with lines

iceberg.name: Iceberg
iceberg.description: Visible portion above waterline contrasting with large hidden mass below
iceberg.sketch_base: Iceberg shape with waterline drawn horizontally, small triangular peak above water line and large bulk below, shading darker underneath

fishbone.name: Fishbone
fishbone.description: Central spine with branching bones showing contributing factors
fishbone.sketch_base: Horizontal spine with angled bones branching upward and downward, labels on each bone, pointed head on right side resembling fish skeleton

funnel.name: Funnel
funnel.description: Wide opening at top narrowing to point, showing filtering or conversion
funnel.sketch_base: Trapezoid shape pointing downward with horizontal segments inside, widest at top narrowing progressively, indicating flow downward

scale_balance.name: Scale Balance
scale_balance.description: Two-pan balance scale showing relative weights and equilibrium
scale_balance.sketch_base: Hand-drawn balance scale with central fulcrum, left and right pans, different sized weights on each side showing imbalance or balance

network_graph.name: Network Graph
network_graph.description: Nodes connected by relationship lines showing interconnected systems
network_graph.sketch_base: Multiple circles or nodes scattered on page connected by lines of varying thickness, some nodes larger than others showing importance or connection density

timeline.name: Timeline
timeline.description: Horizontal or vertical chronological progression marking key events
timeline.sketch_base: Horizontal line with perpendicular tick marks and labels, events marked above or below line, arrows showing progression from left to right

s_curve.name: S-Curve
s_curve.description: S-shaped adoption or growth curve showing slow start, rapid growth, plateau
s_curve.sketch_base: Smooth S-shaped curve starting flat at bottom left, steepening in middle, then flattening at top right, labeled axes for time and adoption percentage

exponential_curve.name: Exponential Curve
exponential_curve.description: Hockey-stick shaped curve showing rapid exponential growth
exponential_curve.sketch_base: Curve starting nearly flat near origin, then sharply rising upward to top right, resembling hockey stick, labeled with growth phases

comparison.name: Comparison
comparison.description: Side-by-side contrast panels showing differences or similarities
comparison.sketch_base: Two equal vertical sections or panels placed side by side with vertical dividing line, different content/shadings in each representing contrasting options

tripod.name: Tripod
tripod.description: Three equal pillars or legs supporting a central beam or platform
tripod.sketch_base: Three hand-drawn diagonal legs meeting at top at angles, supporting horizontal beam or platform on top, each leg labeled with principle

flow_chart.name: Flow Chart
flow_chart.description: Multi-path process diagram with decision points and various flow directions
flow_chart.sketch_base: Multiple rectangular boxes and diamond-shaped decision nodes connected by arrows, arrows labeled with yes/no or conditions, showing complex path options

force_diagram.name: Force Diagram
force_diagram.description: Arrows pushing or pulling on central point showing competing forces
force_diagram.sketch_base: Central circle or object with multiple thick arrows pointing toward or away from it from different directions, some arrows larger than others

spectrum.name: Spectrum
spectrum.description: Gradient continuum from one extreme to another on a linear scale
spectrum.sketch_base: Horizontal bar with gradient coloring from one shade to another, endpoints labeled with opposing concepts, intermediate markers along length

nested_circles.name: Nested Circles
nested_circles.description: Concentric circles showing containment, layers, or levels of abstraction
nested_circles.sketch_base: Multiple hand-drawn circles of increasing size centered on same point, labeled from innermost to outermost with different levels or categories

categories.name: Cognitive Biases
categories.description: Mental shortcuts and systematic distortions in thinking and judgment

categories.name: Systems Thinking
categories.description: Understanding complex systems and their interconnected behaviors

categories.name: Probability & Statistics
categories.description: Mathematical reasoning about uncertainty and data patterns

categories.name: Problem-Solving
categories.description: Systematic approaches to analyzing and solving problems

categories.name: Psychology & Behavioral
categories.description: Human behavior, motivation, and psychological patterns

categories.name: Visualization Frameworks
categories.description: Meta-frameworks for thinking about and presenting ideas

categories.name: Islamic / Quranic
categories.description: Principles and frameworks from Islamic teaching and Quranic wisdom

models.name: Anchoring Bias
models.description: The tendency to rely too heavily on an initial piece of information (the anchor) when making subsequent decisions. Even when the anchor is irrelevant or arbitrary, it influences judgments and valuations.
models.when_to_use: When negotiating, pricing products, making estimates, or evaluating information presented first in a sequence
models.visual_description: Hand-drawn balance scale with a heavy anchor symbol (chain link) on the left pan pulling the scale dramatically downward, while numerical values or arguments on the right pan appear lighter and ineffective at balancing. The fulcrum is slightly off-center to emphasize the anchor's disproportionate influence. Subtle waves or ripples emanate from the anchor to show its gravity. Labels read 'Initial Information (Anchor)' on left and 'Subsequent Values' on right.

models.name: Availability Heuristic
models.description: The tendency to estimate the probability of an event based on how easily examples come to mind. Vivid, recent, or emotionally charged events are overweighted in judgment.
models.when_to_use: When assessing risk, estimating frequency of events, or making judgments about likelihood
models.visual_description: Inverted funnel shape with a bright spotlight shining downward from top, illuminating vivid memories (airplane crash, shark attack) while the darker base of the funnel shows statistically more common events (car accidents, disease) in shadow. The funnel narrows toward the bottom where the most available memories are concentrated and brightest. Numbers on sides show distorted vs actual probabilities. An 'ease of recall' dimension is shown with bold arrows pointing to memorable events.

models.name: Confirmation Bias
models.description: The tendency to search for, interpret, favor, and recall information in a way that confirms one's preexisting beliefs or hypotheses. Contradicting evidence is often overlooked or minimized.
models.when_to_use: When seeking information, evaluating evidence, or holding strong opinions
models.visual_description: Funnel or tunnel viewed from the side with a person's head at the entrance. Information flows in from all directions but only information matching the belief system (shown with matching color/symbols) makes it through the narrow passage, while contradicting information (different colors/symbols) bounces off the tunnel walls or is blocked by a filter membrane. The kept information emerges on the right side of the tunnel in a narrow stream. Labels show 'Incoming Information', 'Belief Filter', and 'Confirmation Stream'.

models.name: Sunk Cost Fallacy
models.description: The tendency to continue investing in something because of previously invested resources (time, money, effort) that cannot be recovered, even when continued investment is not rational.
models.when_to_use: When evaluating whether to quit a project, end a relationship, or abandon a course of action
models.visual_description: A sinking ship illustrated in side view with a person inside who has already thrown considerable treasure/money/effort into the water (shown as a trail of sinking coins and items). The person holds more coins/effort above the water, ready to throw in more to 'recover' the loss. The ship continues sinking despite the investment. Below the waterline are stacked layers of past investments fading into darkness. An arrow labeled 'Rational Decision' points away from the ship toward the lifeboat, while the person's path continues toward deeper water. The past investments appear as chains pulling downward.

models.name: Dunning-Kruger Effect
models.description: A cognitive bias where people with limited knowledge or expertise in a domain substantially overestimate their competence, while those with expertise tend to underestimate their abilities.
models.when_to_use: When observing overconfidence in beginners or understanding why experts are more cautious
models.visual_description: A dual-axis graph showing two curves: Competence Level (x-axis from low to high) and Confidence Level (y-axis). The 'Actual Competence' curve starts low on the left, gradually increases, and plateaus at the top right. The 'Confidence' curve starts high on the left (peak of overconfidence), plummets dramatically in the middle (where knowledge increases and overconfidence crashes), then gradually rises again to match competence on the right. Labels mark the left region as 'Peak of Mt. Stupid' with a figure confidently standing on a small hill. The middle section is labeled 'Valley of Despair' showing realization of knowledge gaps. The right is labeled 'Slope of Enlightenment'.

models.name: Loss Aversion
models.description: The tendency to prefer avoiding losses to acquiring equivalent gains. The pain of losing is psychologically more powerful than the pleasure of winning, roughly twice as strong.
models.when_to_use: When making decisions involving risk, investments, or trade-offs between gains and losses
models.visual_description: Two side-by-side mirror-image curves showing value on the y-axis and gain/loss on the x-axis. The gain side (right) shows a gentle upward curve labeled 'Gain Curve'. The loss side (left) shows a steep downward curve labeled 'Loss Curve' that plummets much more dramatically. The curves create an asymmetric shape. A horizontal reference line at zero shows the asymmetry clearly - the loss side extends much lower than the gain side extends higher. The gap between the curves is labeled '2x ratio' with an arrow. Small illustrations show a smiling person on the gain side and a distressed person on the loss side.

models.name: Hindsight Bias
models.description: The tendency to see past events as more predictable than they actually were. After an outcome is known, people often claim they 'knew it all along', overestimating their prior ability to predict events.
models.when_to_use: When analyzing past events, evaluating decision quality, or learning from mistakes
models.visual_description: Horizontal timeline with Past on the left, Present in middle, and Future on the right. On the left side before a major event (marked with an X), the timeline shows multiple branching paths with different possible futures, all equally visible and unclear. At the event point (X), all possibilities except one are highlighted/emphasized as 'obvious' in hindsight. After the event on the right, a single clear path is shown with markers retroactively placed saying 'obvious', 'predictable', 'should have known' along the actual path. A person stands at the Present looking back with a confident expression despite the past showing genuine uncertainty. The hindsight rewrite is shown in a different color overlaying the original uncertain past.

models.name: Narrative Fallacy
models.description: The tendency to impose narrative structure and causal explanations on random or complex events, creating coherent stories even when underlying data is disconnected or coincidental.
models.when_to_use: When interpreting historical events, analyzing business performance, or explaining coincidences
models.visual_description: On the left side, scattered random dots and disconnected points in space with no visible connections, labeled 'Random Events'. On the right side, the same dots are now connected with flowing curved lines creating an elaborate narrative story (shown as an illustrated story arc or path connecting the points). A story caption or narrative flows above the right side dots. Arrows show how the human brain 'connects' random events into a coherent story. The contrast shows 'What Actually Happened' vs 'The Story We Tell'. Small illustrations show a confused person looking at random dots, then a satisfied person reading a coherent narrative.

models.name: Recency Bias
models.description: The tendency to give more weight and importance to recent events and information than to older events. Recent data disproportionately influences judgment and memory.
models.when_to_use: When evaluating long-term patterns, making investment decisions, or assessing performance
models.visual_description: A horizontal timeline extending from Past (left) to Recent (right) to Future (right). Events along the timeline are shown as circles of varying sizes. Older events (left side) are shown as small, faded circles in light gray. Moving rightward, events gradually increase in size and brightness. Recent events (right side) are enormous, vivid, and brightly colored circles that dominate the visual. A person standing at the Present point looks primarily at the large recent circles while the small past circles fade in their peripheral vision. An arrow labeled 'Weight/Importance' points upward more steeply on the right side. A dotted line shows 'Rational Weight Distribution' as a gentle slope, contrasted with the actual steep slope toward recent events.

models.name: Authority Bias
models.description: The tendency to attribute greater accuracy to the opinion of an authority figure and be more influenced by that opinion, even when the authority lacks relevant expertise.
models.when_to_use: When evaluating advice, making medical or financial decisions, or assessing information credibility
models.visual_description: A large, imposing figure labeled 'Authority' (wearing a crown, robes, or other symbols of authority) stands at the center with a wide shadow cast. Multiple thick arrows emanate outward from the authority figure toward smaller ordinary figures around them, pushing them or pulling their opinions toward the authority. The surrounding people appear smaller and are bending or leaning toward the authority figure despite evidence symbols (books, data points, contradictory information) floating nearby that are being ignored. The authority's words appear in large text bubbles while conflicting evidence appears in tiny, faint text. A halo or glow surrounds the authority figure indicating unwarranted positive bias.

models.name: Status Quo Bias
models.description: The preference for the current state of affairs, with changes perceived as losses rather than gains. People prefer things to remain the same, viewing change with suspicion despite potential benefits.
models.when_to_use: When encouraging organizational change, personal development, or decision-making about alternatives
models.visual_description: A person stands in a comfortable position labeled 'Status Quo' with a heavy anchor chain attached to their feet, firmly rooted to the ground. Around them are visible paths and doors leading to alternatives (labeled 'Better Option A', 'Better Option B'), shown with welcoming light. However, the anchor pulls downward with enormous force, keeping the person in place. Arrows representing 'Change Resistance' point downward from the person toward the anchor. The person looks torn but remains immobilized. The ground around the Status Quo position is shown as quicksand-like, suggesting resistance to movement. Weight measurements show the anchor as disproportionately heavy compared to the person.

models.name: Base Rate Neglect
models.description: The tendency to focus on specific individual information while ignoring the statistical base rate of the general population. Overall probability is underweighted in favor of specific details.
models.when_to_use: When making medical diagnoses, evaluating specific cases, or assessing individual probabilities
models.visual_description: A large pyramid representing a population base (e.g., 1 million people) divided into segments. A small sliver at the top represents a specific condition (disease, characteristic) affecting 1% of the population. A large spotlight shines down on a single individual selected from the small group, illuminating them brightly. Below the pyramid, a smaller inverted pyramid shows statistics: 'Base Rate: 1%' in small text at bottom. But a person making a decision looks only at the spotlit individual and ignores the large pyramid base statistics. When this individual matches a specific description or test result, the person concludes certainty without considering base rate. The visual shows the base rate information 'faded' or ignored while the individual information is 'bright' and emphasized.

models.name: Overconfidence Bias
models.description: The tendency to have excessive faith in the accuracy of one's own judgments, estimates, and predictions, leading to underestimation of uncertainty and risk.
models.when_to_use: When making predictions, planning projects, or assessing your own accuracy
models.visual_description: A confidence meter or gauge on the left showing a needle pointing to 'Very High' (90%), labeled 'Confidence Level'. On the right, an accuracy meter shows the needle pointing to 'Moderate' (60%), labeled 'Actual Accuracy'. The gap between them is highlighted as 'Overconfidence Gap' with double-headed arrow. Below are two zones: a narrow band at the top showing 'Actual Correct Predictions' with few checkmarks, and a wide band below showing 'Predicted Correct But Wrong' with many X marks. A person stands confidently chest-out in front of the confidence meter while looking away from the accuracy meter. Numbers show the math: '90% confidence but only 60% actual accuracy = 30% overconfidence'.

models.name: Framing Effect
models.description: The tendency to react differently to choices depending on whether they are presented in terms of potential gains or potential losses, even when the actual outcomes are identical.
models.when_to_use: When presenting information, making decisions about risk, or understanding marketing messages
models.visual_description: Two identical glass containers showing the exact same amount of liquid inside, but with different frames around them. On the left, the frame is labeled 'Glass is 50% Full' with a happy face, and the visual emphasizes the filled portion (top) with bright green color. On the right, the same container with frame labeled 'Glass is 50% Empty' with a sad face, and the visual emphasizes the empty portion (bottom) with red/dark color. Below are two scenarios: 'Medical Scenario' showing the same statistics presented as '95% success rate' (left, positive frame with checkmarks) versus '5% failure rate' (right, negative frame with X marks). Arrows show people making different decisions despite identical outcomes. The word 'Frame' appears prominently, showing that only the presentation changed, not the reality.

models.name: Survivorship Bias
models.description: The tendency to focus on successful cases that passed some selection process while overlooking those that did not. Systematic bias toward visible successes and away from failures.
models.when_to_use: When analyzing success factors, learning from examples, or understanding selection effects
models.visual_description: A large funnel at the top filled with many colored balls representing all attempts/companies/participants. As the funnel narrows downward, balls drop out the sides (shown as failures disappearing into darkness below). Only a few balls emerge at the bottom of the funnel, labeled 'Survivors/Visible Success Stories'. These survivors are brightly illuminated and displayed prominently with statistics and analysis arrows pointing to them. Below and outside the funnel are shadowy, faded areas labeled 'Invisible Failures' with thousands of failed attempts hidden in darkness. A researcher or analyst focuses only on the survivors at the funnel bottom, extracting 'lessons' and 'success principles' while the much larger failure population remains invisible and unconsidered. The visual emphasizes the selection effect with an arrow showing how only visible successes are studied.

models.name: Feedback Loops
models.description: Circular causal relationships where changes in one variable influence other variables, which in turn influence the original variable, creating reinforcing or balancing cycles.
models.when_to_use: When analyzing system behavior, understanding escalation patterns, or predicting long-term outcomes
models.visual_description: Two side-by-side circular diagrams. Left circle shows 'Reinforcing (Positive) Feedback Loop': Large curved arrow flows clockwise through stages (e.g., 'Success' → 'Confidence' → 'Better Decisions' → 'More Success'), with each stage shown in progressively larger circles, growing outward. Plus signs (+) mark each connection. The overall effect shows exponential growth or spiral upward. Right circle shows 'Balancing (Negative) Feedback Loop': Arrow flows clockwise but stages alternate between opposing forces (e.g., 'Heat Up' → 'Thermostat Triggers' → 'Cool Down' → 'Stop Cooling' → repeats), shown as same-sized circles in equilibrium. Minus signs (-) mark some connections. Arrows point outward from the loops showing their effects. Colors differentiate the two types: warm colors for reinforcing, cool colors for balancing.

models.name: Second Order Thinking
models.description: The practice of thinking beyond the immediate consequences of an action to consider indirect effects, feedback loops, and long-term outcomes. Asking 'and then what?'
models.when_to_use: When making major decisions, implementing policies, or planning long-term strategy
models.visual_description: A multi-level consequence tree starting with a single action at the left (e.g., 'Reduce Interest Rates'). The first level of consequences branches rightward (e.g., 'Cheaper Borrowing', 'Reduced Savings'). The second level branches further from each consequence with additional effects (e.g., from cheaper borrowing: 'More Consumption', 'More Debt'). The third and fourth levels continue branching, showing increasingly indirect and sometimes unintended consequences. The branches are colored in layers: first-level consequences in one color, second-order in another, third and fourth orders in progressively different shades. Some branches curve back to create feedback loops. Labels show: '1st Order: Direct Effect', '2nd Order: Indirect Effects', '3rd Order: Side Effects', '4th Order: Unintended Consequences'. A person analyzing the tree moves from left to right, initially seeing only the first branch, then expanding vision to see all the cascading effects.

models.name: Tipping Point
models.description: A critical threshold where a small change in input produces a large, often sudden and non-linear change in output or system behavior. The point of no return in a transition.
models.when_to_use: When understanding social movements, predicting sudden shifts, or analyzing critical thresholds
models.visual_description: An S-shaped curve on x-y axes labeled 'Cause' (x-axis) and 'Effect' (y-axis). The curve starts nearly flat at bottom left through the first third (labeled 'Accumulation Phase'). A vertical threshold line marks the tipping point at the curve's steepest section. Before the threshold, the curve is nearly horizontal showing minimal change despite input. At the tipping point, a bright red line shows where dramatic change occurs. After the threshold, the curve flattens again at the top right (labeled 'New Equilibrium'). Small illustrations show: before tipping point - a small push has minimal effect; at tipping point - a tiny push causes dramatic cascade; after tipping point - system at new stable state. The area around the threshold is highlighted with a warning color. An iceberg is shown at the tipping point moment, balanced precariously, about to tip.

models.name: Stock and Flow
models.description: A system of accumulation and depletion where stock is the quantity accumulated over time and flow is the rate at which stock changes. Understanding this separation is key to system dynamics.
models.when_to_use: When analyzing accumulation over time, understanding rates of change, or modeling dynamic systems
models.visual_description: A large cylindrical tank or reservoir labeled 'Stock' positioned in the center. On the left side, a thick inflow pipe labeled 'Inflow' carries water/liquid into the tank with arrows showing direction and speed. Measurement gauge on the inflow shows 'Flow Rate: Amount per Time Unit'. On the right side, an outflow pipe drains water out with similar measurement gauges. The tank contains shaded liquid representing accumulated stock, with a level indicator on the side showing current stock quantity. Below the pipes are time-series graphs showing how inflow and outflow rates change over time, with the tank stock level shown as the integral (area under the curves). Labels show the distinction: 'Stock = Accumulated Quantity', 'Inflow = Rate In', 'Outflow = Rate Out'. An equation appears: 'Stock Change = Inflow - Outflow'. Multiple time snapshots show the stock quantity changing as flows continue.

models.name: System Delays
models.description: Time lags between cause and effect in a system, where the impact of an action is not immediately visible. These delays often lead to overcompensation and instability.
models.when_to_use: When understanding why systems overshoot, why delayed feedback causes problems, or predicting lag effects
models.visual_description: A horizontal timeline showing a person taking an action on the left. An arrow labeled 'Cause/Action' originates from the person (e.g., 'Turn up heating'). The timeline extends rightward with a large gap labeled 'DELAY - System Response Time'. During this delay period, the action's effect is invisible, shown as a faded or ghosted area. Finally, the actual effect appears on the right side of the timeline (e.g., 'Room temperature rises'), but by then considerable time has passed and the person may have already taken additional actions, not realizing their first action is now kicking in. A feedback arrow shows the delayed response arriving late. Below the timeline are two scenarios: one where the delay causes overcorrection (person takes extra action before feeling the first effect), and another showing proper response with adequate delay awareness. The visual emphasizes invisible causes during the delay period.

models.name: Leverage Points
models.description: Places in a system where a small amount of force or change can produce disproportionately large effects. High-leverage interventions multiply impact.
models.when_to_use: When seeking to influence a system efficiently, allocating limited resources, or maximizing impact
models.visual_description: A simple lever balanced on a fulcrum (shown as triangular pivot point). A large weight (representing system resistance) is placed far from the fulcrum on one side. A small hand applies downward force (representing intervention effort) on the other side of the lever, much closer to the fulcrum. Despite the small effort input, the weight is lifted dramatically on the opposite side, demonstrating mechanical advantage. Distance markers show the leverage ratio (e.g., '1:10 effort to effect'). Multiple additional examples show different systems with leverage points marked: a small stone placed at a specific point prevents a large boulder from rolling, a small change in a critical parameter affects the entire system, a single influential person changes group dynamics. The visual emphasizes 'Place of Intervention' with arrows pointing to high-leverage spots. A comparison shows ineffective intervention (pushing the heavy weight directly) versus effective (using the leverage point).

models.name: Emergence
models.description: The appearance of complex patterns, properties, or behaviors that arise from relatively simple individual components interacting with each other. The whole is greater than the sum of parts.
models.when_to_use: When understanding how simple rules create complexity, analyzing collective behavior, or designing adaptive systems
models.visual_description: Bottom of the image shows many small identical shapes (e.g., birds shown as simple circles) scattered randomly with simple directional arrows showing local interaction rules (stay close to neighbor, match velocity, avoid collision). In the middle level, these individual shapes cluster and align, creating visible patterns of movement. At the top of the image, the emergent property becomes clearly visible: a coherent flock of birds swirling in a complex, beautiful pattern that could not be predicted from looking at a single bird's rules. The complexity increases vertically. Labels show: 'Level 1: Simple Individual Rules', 'Level 2: Local Interactions', 'Level 3: Emergent Complex Behavior'. Additional examples show: ant colonies following pheromone trails creating complex logistics; slime molds organizing without central control; neurons in the brain creating consciousness. Arrows show the principle: 'Simple Rules + Many Interactions = Unexpected Complexity'.

models.name: Butterfly Effect
models.description: The phenomenon where very small changes in initial conditions can produce dramatically different outcomes in complex systems. A butterfly flapping its wings in Brazil could theoretically cause a tornado in Texas.
models.when_to_use: When understanding sensitivity to initial conditions, chaos in systems, or unpredictability of complex outcomes
models.visual_description: A tiny butterfly at origin point on the left shown with delicate, small wings. A small arrow labeled 'Tiny Initial Condition' emerges from the butterfly. This arrow splits and branches into multiple paths, each path curving and diverging from the others. As the paths extend rightward across the image, they separate more dramatically, creating an expanding fan shape. At the right side, one path leads to a peaceful landscape (no tornado), another leads to a dramatic tornado scene, another to a calm weather pattern. The visual emphasizes that identical initial conditions (butterfly wing flap) could deterministically lead to completely different outcomes. A graph below shows 'Outcome Divergence' over time, starting nearly identical but rapidly spreading apart. Contrast images show: tiny difference in input (one butterfly wing position) creating completely different weather systems output. The butterfly is drawn delicately, emphasizing how minute the initial cause is compared to the massive effect.

models.name: Bayesian Thinking
models.description: A method of updating beliefs based on new evidence using conditional probability. Prior beliefs are refined by incorporating new information to arrive at posterior probabilities.
models.when_to_use: When updating beliefs with new evidence, making medical diagnoses, or evaluating hypothesis plausibility
models.visual_description: On the left, a prior probability distribution shown as a wide bell curve labeled 'Prior Belief' representing initial uncertainty. Below shows 'Prior: 20% Chance Disease'. New evidence arrives (shown as data points or test results illustrated on the left). The diagram branches rightward showing 'Bayes Theorem: P(A|B) = P(B|A)×P(A)/P(B)' with variables labeled. The curve updates and narrows rightward in response to the evidence, shown as progressively narrower distributions. After the first evidence, the curve is narrower (labeled 'Posterior 1: 35% Chance'). Additional evidence continues the cycle, further narrowing the distribution rightward (labeled 'Posterior 2: 60% Chance', 'Posterior 3: 78% Chance'). The visual shows how prior belief + evidence = updated belief. Color coding shows: initial belief in one color, evidence in another, updated belief in a third. The cycle can be repeated: new posterior becomes prior for the next update.

models.name: Expected Value
models.description: The anticipated value of an outcome calculated by multiplying the value of each possible outcome by its probability and summing the results. A rational basis for decision-making.
models.when_to_use: When evaluating risky decisions, gambling odds, investment returns, or comparing uncertain outcomes
models.visual_description: A table or grid showing multiple possible outcomes in rows. Each row shows: 'Outcome A' with value $100 and probability 40%, 'Outcome B' with value $50 and probability 30%, 'Outcome C' with value -$20 and probability 30%. Next to each is a calculation: '$100 × 0.40 = $40', '$50 × 0.30 = $15', '-$20 × 0.30 = -$6'. A sum bar at the bottom shows 'Expected Value = $40 + $15 + (-$6) = $49'. Visual elements show multiplying outcome values by probability bars (wide bars for high probability, narrow for low). A comparison shows: 'Biased Decision (ignore probability)' making the high-value outcome choice versus 'Rational Decision (use expected value)' choosing the option with highest expected value. The visual emphasizes that expected value accounts for both magnitude and likelihood.

models.name: Black Swan
models.description: A rare, unpredictable event with extreme consequences that lies outside the range of normal expectations. Events previously thought impossible but with significant impact when they occur.
models.when_to_use: When assessing tail risks, building resilience, or understanding rare event impacts
models.visual_description: A normal distribution curve shown as a bell curve centered in the image, labeled 'Expected Events'. The vast majority of the curve area is shaded in light blue and labeled 'What Actually Happens 99% of Time'. On the far right tail of the distribution, an extremely small and exaggerated tail extends outward, shown in dark red/black. Within this tail sits a single black swan illustration, looking exotic and rare, labeled 'Black Swan Event: Extreme, Rare, Unpredicted'. The tail is drawn disproportionately large compared to its actual probability to emphasize the impact. A vertical line separates the normal distribution from the tail. Below the curve, a timeline shows: 'Normal Years' with small events, then suddenly 'Black Swan Event - Everything Changes'. Examples are labeled: 'COVID-19', '9/11', 'Financial Crisis 2008'. The visual emphasizes unpredictability (the event wasn't visible in prior patterns) and outsized impact.

models.name: Regression to the Mean
models.description: The statistical phenomenon where extreme values tend to move closer to the average in subsequent measurements. Regression occurs regardless of the cause of the initial extreme value.
models.when_to_use: When analyzing repeated measurements, evaluating treatment effectiveness, or understanding performance fluctuations
models.visual_description: A horizontal timeline showing data points plotted as dots or circles. On the left side, one or two points are shown far above average (labeled 'Extreme High Point'). A horizontal dashed line runs through the middle labeled 'Average/Mean'. Moving rightward in time, subsequent measurements (shown as a series of points) gradually move down and closer to the average line (shown with arrows indicating downward movement). The convergence toward the mean is gradual over several measurements, eventually settling near the average line. A bell curve appears in the background showing the distribution these points are drawn from. Labels show: 'Extreme Event', 'Regression Period', 'Return to Mean'. A commentary below explains: 'High performers regress to mean' and 'This happens with NO intervention.' The visual emphasizes that regression occurs naturally due to the randomness that created the extreme in the first place.

models.name: Normal Distribution
models.description: The bell-shaped probability distribution that appears frequently in nature and statistics, where most values cluster around the mean with fewer values at the extremes.
models.when_to_use: When analyzing data patterns, calculating probability intervals, or understanding likelihood of values
models.visual_description: A large bell curve drawn symmetrically, centered on the mean. Vertical lines mark standard deviations from the mean: 1 SD, 2 SD, 3 SD on each side. The areas under the curve are shaded to show percentages: 68% within 1 SD (dark shade), 95% within 2 SD (medium shade), 99.7% within 3 SD (light shade). Below the curve, a ruler shows the measurement scale with the mean at center. Labels indicate: 'Mean = Center', 'Standard Deviation = Spread', 'Probability = Area Under Curve'. Example data points are shown as dots scattered on the curve, denser near the center, sparse at tails. A comparison example shows: 'Heights of Adult Men' with the bell curve, or 'Test Scores' with similar distribution. The x-axis is labeled 'Value' and y-axis labeled 'Frequency/Probability Density'.

models.name: Tail Risk
models.description: The probability and impact of extreme, rare, and unpredictable events at the edges of a probability distribution. Tail events are often underestimated in risk assessment.
models.when_to_use: When assessing comprehensive risk, building robust strategies, or understanding worst-case scenarios
models.visual_description: A normal distribution curve with the main body highlighted in light blue labeled 'Center: Expected Risk'. At both far right and far left edges, the distribution extends into red-shaded 'tail' regions labeled 'Tail Risk: Rare but Extreme'. The tails are drawn emphasizing their thin, extended nature. A zoomed-in inset shows a magnified view of the tail region with examples of tail events: '-5 standard deviations: Market Crash', '-4 SD: Political Crisis', '+4 SD: Black Swan Opportunity'. Below the curve, a risk intensity gradient shows the tails as extremely dangerous (dark red) while the center is manageable (blue). Arrows from the tails point outward to scenarios showing catastrophic consequences. A comparison shows: 'Typical Risk Assessment (ignores tails)' with a small bell curve, versus 'Comprehensive Risk (includes tails)' with the same curve but with large red arrows extending from tails.

models.name: Conditional Probability
models.description: The probability of an event occurring given that another event has already occurred. Represents the relationship between dependent events.
models.when_to_use: When analyzing sequential events, medical testing, or understanding dependencies between outcomes
models.visual_description: A tree structure showing an initial event at the root: 'Event A Occurs' (shown as a branch splitting from left). This branch divides into two sub-branches labeled 'Event B Happens' and 'Event B Doesn't Happen' with percentages. Below the first branch shows: 'P(B|A) = Probability of B Given A Already Occurred'. The percentages on these branches show how the probability of B changes depending on whether A occurred. In contrast, another tree section shows Event A not occurring, with a different probability for Event B in that scenario. The visual demonstrates: 'P(B|A) ≠ P(B)' - the probability of B depends on whether A happened. A practical example shows: medical testing scenario where probability of having disease (B) depends on test result (A). Colors differentiate the paths, with labels showing the calculation: 'P(B|A) = P(A and B) / P(A)'.

models.name: Pareto Principle (80/20)
models.description: The observation that roughly 80% of effects come from 20% of causes. A powerful principle for prioritization showing that inputs and outputs are often not uniformly distributed.
models.when_to_use: When allocating resources, prioritizing efforts, or identifying high-impact activities
models.visual_description: A horizontal bar divided into 10 equal segments. The first 2 segments (20%) are highlighted in bright green and labeled 'Vital Few: 20% of Efforts'. These 2 segments are drawn much taller/larger. The remaining 8 segments (80%) are shown in light gray and labeled 'Trivial Many: 80% of Efforts' drawn proportionally shorter. Below, an inverse bar shows results: the same 2 segments (20%) produce 80% of the results (shown in large green area), while the 8 segments (80%) produce only 20% of results (shown in small gray area). The visual clearly shows disproportionate relationship. Additional examples are labeled: 'Sales: 20% of customers = 80% of revenue', 'Quality: 20% of bugs = 80% of problems', 'Time: 20% of tasks = 80% of impact'. An arrow emphasizes: 'Focus resources here!' pointing to the vital 20%. The contrast between effort distribution (uniform) and result distribution (skewed) is the key insight.

models.name: Opportunity Cost
models.description: The value of the next best alternative foregone when choosing one option. The cost of choice is not just the money spent, but what you give up by not choosing something else.
models.when_to_use: When making important decisions, evaluating trade-offs, or understanding true cost of choices
models.visual_description: A fork in the road shown at the center. The left path shows one decision (e.g., 'Start a Business') highlighted in bright color, with positive outcomes listed (freedom, potential earnings). This path is strongly illuminated. The right path shows the alternative choice (e.g., 'Stay in Current Job') faded and ghosted, with the outcomes listed but shown in diminished/grayed out manner to indicate what was sacrificed. A cost label appears between the paths showing: 'Opportunity Cost of Left Path = Benefits of Right Path Given Up'. The visual emphasizes that choosing the left path means losing the right path's benefits forever. Multiple examples are shown: 'Choosing Investment A means losing returns from Investment B', 'Time spent on Task X is time not spent on Task Y'. The further along the chosen path you go, the more faded the other path becomes, showing increasing commitment. A timeline shows the opportunity cost accumulating over time.

models.name: Supply and Demand
models.description: The fundamental economic model where price equilibrium is determined by the intersection of supply curve (quantity suppliers willing to provide) and demand curve (quantity consumers willing to buy).
models.when_to_use: When analyzing markets, predicting price movements, or understanding market dynamics
models.visual_description: An x-y graph with Price on the y-axis and Quantity on the x-axis. A downward-sloping line labeled 'Demand Curve' starts at top-left and descends to bottom-right, showing higher price = lower quantity demanded. An upward-sloping line labeled 'Supply Curve' starts at bottom-left and rises to top-right, showing higher price = higher quantity supplied. The two curves intersect at a point marked with a circle and labeled 'Market Equilibrium: Price & Quantity'. At equilibrium, quantity supplied equals quantity demanded. The area between the curves on the left (above equilibrium) is labeled 'Shortage: Price Pressure Up', shaded in red. The area on the right (below equilibrium) is labeled 'Surplus: Price Pressure Down', shaded in blue. Arrows show market forces pushing toward equilibrium. Additional labels show: examples of what shifts each curve (e.g., 'Consumer Preference Change' shifts demand, 'Technology Improvement' shifts supply). The visual emphasizes the self-correcting nature of markets.

models.name: Network Effects
models.description: The phenomenon where the value of a product or service increases as more people use it. Each new user creates positive externalities for existing users.
models.when_to_use: When analyzing platform businesses, understanding competitive advantages, or predicting technology adoption
models.visual_description: On the left, a single node labeled 'User 1' is shown isolated. Moving rightward: 'User 2 Joins' creating a connection line between them (value = 1 connection). Next: 'User 3 Joins' creating multiple new connections (value = 3 total connections shown). Continuing rightward: each new user creates exponentially more connections. The network expands visually with increasingly dense connection patterns. Below the network, a value graph shows an exponential curve: 'Value per User' starts flat, then curves upward sharply as more users join. The growth is labeled as non-linear. Examples are shown: 'Telephone: Valuable when many can call', 'Social Media: Useful with network of friends', 'Marketplace: Better with many buyers and sellers'. A section shows 'Critical Mass Point' marked on the value curve - before this point, growth is slow; after this point, exponential growth. The visual emphasizes the virtuous cycle: More Users → More Value → Attracts More Users.

models.name: Porter's Five Forces
models.description: A framework analyzing competitive pressure in an industry through five forces: supplier power, buyer power, competitive rivalry, threat of substitutes, and threat of new entrants.
models.when_to_use: When analyzing industry attractiveness, developing competitive strategy, or evaluating business positioning
models.visual_description: A central circle labeled 'Industry/Company' positioned in the center. Five thick arrows point toward this center from different directions, each labeled with one force: 'Threat of New Entrants' from top, 'Supplier Power' from left, 'Buyer Power' from right, 'Threat of Substitutes' from bottom. A fifth force arrow labeled 'Competitive Rivalry' points horizontally from the left, emphasizing intense rivalry between existing competitors. Each arrow's thickness indicates the force's strength - thicker arrows indicate stronger forces. Colors differentiate the forces. Below the diagram, a legend shows what factors determine each force: 'New Entrants: Barriers to entry, capital requirements', 'Suppliers: Concentration, switching costs', 'Buyers: Price sensitivity, alternatives', 'Substitutes: Availability, relative value', 'Rivalry: Competitor count, product differentiation'. The overall pressure on the central circle is calculated as the sum of all five forces, shown with a pressure meter.

models.name: Game Theory
models.description: The mathematical study of strategic interaction between rational players, analyzing how individual choices create collective outcomes and identifying dominant strategies.
models.when_to_use: When analyzing strategic interactions, predicting competitor behavior, or designing incentives
models.visual_description: A 2x2 matrix grid with 'Player 1 Strategies' on the left (rows: 'Cooperate'/'Defect') and 'Player 2 Strategies' on top (columns: 'Cooperate'/'Defect'). Each cell contains payoff pairs showing outcomes for both players (e.g., 'Player 1 gets 3, Player 2 gets 3'). The cells are color-coded: green for mutually good outcomes, red for poor outcomes, yellow for asymmetric outcomes. The cell labeled 'Mutual Cooperation' is highlighted with a positive glow. However, an arrow or emphasis shows how 'Mutual Defection' (often the Nash Equilibrium) traps both players in a suboptimal outcome despite cooperation being better. Labels show: 'Payoff for Player 1' and 'Payoff for Player 2' for each outcome. Below the matrix, notation appears: 'Nash Equilibrium = Strategy where no player benefits from unilateral change'. The visual demonstrates the strategic tension in game theory.

models.name: Prisoner's Dilemma
models.description: A game theory scenario where two rational players pursuing self-interest end up in a worse outcome than if they had cooperated. Individual incentives conflict with collective welfare.
models.when_to_use: When analyzing conflict, cooperation incentives, or tragic commons situations
models.visual_description: Two figures representing prisoners in separate cells stand on opposite sides of a 2x2 matrix. The matrix shows outcomes: 'Both Cooperate (Silent)' = 1 year each (best outcome, shown in green), 'One Defects (Betrays)' = 0 years for betrayer, 10 years for cooperator (worst asymmetric outcome shown in red), 'Both Defect (Betray)' = 5 years each (bad compromise, shown in orange). Each prisoner faces the same incentive: betray, and if the other cooperates you go free; if they also betray, you get 5 years instead of 1. This creates the dilemma where 'Defect' dominates 'Cooperate' individually, yet mutual cooperation gives better results. The visual shows the prisoners unable to communicate, creating the trap. Labels show: 'Individual Incentive: Defect', 'Collective Incentive: Cooperate'. An arrow labeled 'Nash Equilibrium: Both Defect' points to the orange (5,5) cell, showing how rational individual choices lead to a collectively worse outcome.

models.name: Comparative Advantage
models.description: The economic principle that entities benefit from specializing in activities where they have a relative cost advantage compared to others, even if they're absolutely better at everything.
models.when_to_use: When deciding specialization, understanding trade benefits, or allocating tasks among team members
models.visual_description: Two producers (e.g., 'Person A' and 'Person B') shown side-by-side. Horizontal bars show production possibilities: Person A can produce '10 Widgets OR 5 Gadgets per day' (shown as two bars of different lengths). Person B can produce '6 Widgets OR 4 Gadgets per day' (shown with shorter bars). Person A is better at both (absolute advantage). However, the visual shows their relative efficiency: Person A gives up 2 Widgets to make 1 Gadget (cost ratio shown), while Person B gives up 1.5 Widgets to make 1 Gadget. This means Person B has comparative advantage in Gadgets. An arrow shows specialization: Person A focuses on Widgets (draws 10), Person B focuses on Gadgets (draws 4). They trade: A gives Widgets to B, B gives Gadgets to A. The total output is greater than if both made both products. Below shows: 'Without Trade: A makes 8W+2G, B makes 4W+2G = 12W+4G Total' versus 'With Trade: A makes 10W (trades 6W for 3G from B) = 4W+3G, B makes 4G (trades 3G for 6W from A) = 6W+1G = 10W+4G' showing efficiency gain.

models.name: Diminishing Returns
models.description: The phenomenon where increasing inputs of one factor (while others remain constant) eventually produces successively smaller increases in output. Marginal return decreases as input increases.
models.when_to_use: When optimizing resource allocation, understanding productivity limits, or planning expansion
models.visual_description: An output-vs-input graph where the x-axis shows 'Input Amount' (e.g., Marketing Budget) and y-axis shows 'Output' (e.g., Sales). The curve starts at origin and rises steeply on the left (labeled 'Increasing Returns: Each additional input produces large output gains'). The curve's slope gradually decreases through the middle section (labeled 'Diminishing Returns: Additional input produces smaller gains'). Eventually at the right end, the curve nearly flattens (labeled 'Severe Diminishing Returns: Adding more has minimal impact'). Below the curve, a 'Marginal Return' graph shows the incremental benefit per unit of input, starting high on the left and declining downward to nearly zero on the right. Labels show example: 'First Marketing Dollar: 100 sales boost', 'Tenth Dollar: 50 sales boost', 'Hundredth Dollar: 5 sales boost', 'Thousandth Dollar: 0.5 sales boost'. A vertical line marked 'Optimal Point' shows where diminishing returns begin to dominate, suggesting where to stop increasing input.

models.name: Economies of Scale
models.description: The cost advantage achieved when production volume increases, allowing fixed costs to be spread over more units, reducing per-unit cost. Opposite of diminishing returns.
models.when_to_use: When planning expansion, analyzing competitive advantages, or understanding business scalability
models.visual_description: A per-unit cost curve on y-axis ('Cost per Unit') versus production volume on x-axis ('Production Volume'). The curve starts high on the left (labeled 'Small Scale: High Cost per Unit') and steadily declines rightward. The slope shows steep decline initially (labeled 'Strong Economies of Scale'), then gradually flattens out (labeled 'Weak Economies of Scale') as volume increases. At the far right, the curve levels off at a minimum point (labeled 'Optimal Scale'). Below the curve, visual examples show: small production run with high per-unit cost, medium production with lower per-unit cost, large production with lowest per-unit cost. An explanation shows fixed costs (e.g., factory setup = $1M) spread over more units: '1 unit = $1M cost', '1M units = $1 cost'. The curve demonstrates why larger producers have cost advantage over smaller competitors making the same product. A competitive dynamic is shown where one large competitor undercuts smaller ones due to lower per-unit costs.

models.name: First Principles
models.description: The process of breaking down complex problems into fundamental truths and rebuilding from ground up, rather than reasoning by analogy or existing assumptions.
models.when_to_use: When facing novel problems, designing new solutions, or challenging conventional wisdom
models.visual_description: A large complex problem shown at the top (e.g., 'Electric Vehicles Cost Too Much'). Arrows point downward from this problem into a decomposition tree. The tree breaks the problem into component parts: 'Battery = Most Expensive Part', 'Labor = High Cost', 'Materials = Supply Chain Expensive', 'Manufacturing = Inefficient Process'. Each of these breaks down further into more fundamental elements: Battery breaks into 'Lithium Cost', 'Production Efficiency', 'Chemistry', etc. The bottom tier shows the most fundamental truths and atomic elements. Then, the decomposition reverses: arrows point back upward from the fundamental truths, reconstructing solutions. At each level going upward, new possibilities emerge that weren't visible from analogy (e.g., 'If we mine lithium efficiently, if we redesign manufacturing, if we change chemistry... then cost drops significantly'). The visual contrasts the typical approach (top: 'Other companies charge $50k, so we charge $50k') versus first principles (bottom up: 'From fundamentals, cost should be $30k'). The pyramid structure emphasizes building from a solid foundation of truth.

models.name: Inversion Thinking
models.description: Approaching a problem by thinking about its opposite or inverse, which often reveals insights that forward thinking misses. 'How to fail?' instead of 'How to succeed?'
models.when_to_use: When stuck on a problem, planning risk mitigation, or generating creative solutions
models.visual_description: On the left, a person faces a goal labeled 'Success' with a question 'How to achieve success?' surrounded by many possible forward-path arrows spreading rightward, creating complexity and confusion (many ideas competing). On the right, the same person flips around 180 degrees, now asking 'How to guarantee failure?' Arrows now point leftward from the goal to clear, obvious failure modes (shown as specific dangers: 'Ignore customer needs', 'Poor quality', 'Bad timing', 'No planning'). The failure modes are stark, obvious, and limited in number. Below both sides, insights are shown: the success side shows diffuse, hard-to-prioritize success factors, while the failure side clearly identifies what NOT to do. The person then inverts the failure modes to find success strategies. A key insight box shows: 'If we reverse every failure mode, we get success strategy.' The visual emphasizes that knowing what to avoid is sometimes clearer than knowing what to do.

models.name: Occam's Razor
models.description: The principle that simpler explanations are generally better than more complex ones. The explanation requiring the fewest assumptions is usually the correct one.
models.when_to_use: When choosing between competing explanations, simplifying processes, or cutting unnecessary complexity
models.visual_description: On the left, 'Complex Explanation': a tangled web of interconnected lines, boxes, and assumptions (shown with multiple 'If A then B then C then D...' branching paths). The complexity creates confusion visualized as a knot. On the right, 'Simple Explanation': the same phenomenon explained with just a few clear elements connected directly. A large razor is illustrated cutting away the extraneous assumptions/complexity from the left side, leaving only essentials on the right. Below both: the same outcome is achieved, but the right path requires fewer assumptions (shown as a list of 2-3 assumptions vs 10+ assumptions on the left). Labels show: 'Left: 12 Assumptions' vs 'Right: 2 Assumptions'. An arrow labeled 'Occam's Razor: Cut Unnecessary Assumptions' sweeps across, demonstrating the principle. A note explains: 'Simpler explanation requiring fewer assumptions usually correct.' A 'Probability Bar' shows the simpler explanation having higher likelihood of being true.

models.name: Root Cause Analysis (5 Whys)
models.description: A problem-solving technique that repeatedly asks 'Why?' to drill down from symptoms to root causes, typically requiring 5 iterations to reach fundamental causes.
models.when_to_use: When investigating failures, understanding problems deeply, or preventing recurrence
models.visual_description: A vertical drilling-down visualization starting at the top with a Problem statement (e.g., 'Product Launch Failed'). Below are 5 stacked horizontal layers, each asking 'Why?' and drilling deeper: Layer 1: 'Why did it fail? → Market timing wrong' with explanation. Layer 2: 'Why was timing wrong? → Competitor launched first' with explanation. Layer 3: 'Why did competitor launch first? → Our team delayed development' with explanation. Layer 4: 'Why did team delay? → Key engineer left project' with explanation. Layer 5: 'Why did engineer leave? → Working conditions poor, no growth path' - this is the ROOT CAUSE. The visual shows a narrowing shaft drilling downward, with surfaces/debris falling away at each level (representing superficial causes that are stripped away). The shaft penetrates through layers: 'Surface Problem', 'First Why', 'Second Why', 'Third Why', 'Fourth Why', 'Root Cause'. At the bottom, the root cause is illuminated and labeled as the actionable truth. A comparison shows: addressing layer 1 (symptom) won't prevent recurrence, but addressing layer 5 (root) does.

models.name: Fishbone (Ishikawa) Diagram
models.description: A visual diagram for organizing potential causes of a problem into categories, helping identify root causes systematically and comprehensively.
models.when_to_use: When analyzing problems with multiple contributing factors, organizing brainstorms, or investigating quality issues
models.visual_description: A fishbone skeleton shape with the head on the right pointing to a large problem statement box labeled 'Problem: Product Defect'. The main spine (central horizontal line) extends leftward. Major bones branch upward and downward from the spine at angles, each labeled with a category: 'People', 'Methods', 'Materials', 'Machines', 'Environment', 'Management'. Smaller bones branch off each major bone, with specific factors listed (e.g., under 'People': 'Training', 'Experience', 'Motivation'; under 'Machines': 'Calibration', 'Maintenance', 'Age'). Each contributing factor is drawn as a small bone branching into the categorical bones. The entire structure resembles a fish skeleton, with ribs pointing toward the spine and ultimate problem. Colors differentiate major categories. A visual emphasis shows how each small factor contributes to the major category, which contributes to the main problem. The visual is designed to ensure no cause is overlooked, as the structure systematically covers all major categories.

models.name: SCAMPER
models.description: A creative thinking technique using seven prompts (Substitute, Combine, Adapt, Modify, Put to another use, Eliminate, Reverse) to generate innovative ideas and improvements.
models.when_to_use: When brainstorming improvements, designing new products, or finding creative solutions
models.visual_description: A central product or concept shown as a circle in the middle (e.g., 'Bicycle'). Seven spokes extend outward from the center like flower petals, each labeled with a SCAMPER letter and word: 'S-Substitute', 'C-Combine', 'A-Adapt', 'M-Modify', 'P-Put to another use', 'E-Eliminate', 'R-Reverse'. At the end of each spoke is a bulbous petal containing brainstorm ideas responding to that prompt: under 'S-Substitute': 'What if wheels were square?', 'Replace rubber with foam?'; under 'C-Combine': 'Bicycle + Boat = Amphibious bike?'; under 'M-Modify': 'Make it smaller, larger, lighter?'; under 'E-Eliminate': 'Remove pedals, chain, seat?'; under 'R-Reverse': 'Pedals push forward instead of down?'. Each petal contains 2-3 example questions or ideas. Colors differentiate each petal. Arrows show how each prompt generates multiple ideas. The visual emphasizes systematic creativity, ensuring all creative angles are explored.

models.name: Constraint Theory (Theory of Constraints)
models.description: The management principle that any complex system has a bottleneck or constraint that limits overall performance, and optimizing non-constraint areas wastes effort.
models.when_to_use: When optimizing processes, increasing throughput, or improving system performance
models.visual_description: A chain illustrated horizontally with multiple links of equal size, but one link is shown significantly thinner and drawn in a different (weaker) color, representing the constraint. An arrow labeled 'System Throughput' passes through the chain, but its width is restricted to the size of the weakest link - demonstrating that the chain's strength is limited by the weakest link. Additional links to the left and right of the weak link are shown as robust (thick, strong colored), with labels showing 'Optimizing here wastes effort' for the non-constraint links. The weak link is labeled 'Constraint/Bottleneck: Focus here'. A comparison shows: trying to strengthen non-constraint links does nothing to overall chain strength, but strengthening the constraint link increases total chain strength proportionally. Below the chain, a visual shows resource allocation: a pie chart showing 'Typical Resource Allocation' spread across all areas equally, versus 'Optimal Resource Allocation' heavily weighted to the constraint area. Numbers show the impact: 'Improving non-constraints = 1% improvement', 'Improving constraint = 20% improvement'.

models.name: Lateral Thinking
models.description: A thinking method that approaches problems from unconventional angles, avoiding direct linear paths to solutions. Emphasizes creativity and non-traditional reasoning.
models.when_to_use: When stuck with conventional approaches, seeking innovation, or facing problems resistant to logical analysis
models.visual_description: A problem shown on the left. Straight paths extending rightward represent 'Logical/Direct Approaches' (labeled 'Linear Thinking Path') - these take predictable routes but often hit dead-ends or obvious solutions. Curved, spiraling, and angled paths represent 'Lateral Approaches' - these take unexpected routes around obstacles. A large obstacle/wall is drawn in the middle, shown directly in the linear path (blocking it), but the lateral paths circle around it from unexpected angles. The lateral paths eventually reach the goal from different directions than the linear path would. Some lateral paths shown: going upward (above the problem), downward (below), around the back. Each lateral path is labeled with a creative reframing: 'What if we ignore this constraint?', 'What if we combined opposites?', 'What if we approached from the opposite direction?'. The visual shows that lateral thinking generates multiple novel solutions versus the single (possibly blocked) linear solution. A comparison shows: linear thinking = convergent (narrowing to single answer), lateral thinking = divergent (expanding to multiple answers).

models.name: System 1 vs System 2 Thinking
models.description: Two distinct cognitive systems: System 1 is fast, automatic, emotional; System 2 is slow, deliberate, logical. Most thinking is System 1, but System 2 is needed for complex decisions.
models.when_to_use: When understanding decision flaws, improving judgment, or allocating cognitive effort appropriately
models.visual_description: Two side-by-side illustrated pathways. Left side shows 'System 1: Fast Thinking' as a wide, smooth, well-lit highway with a person driving fast (eyes forward, relaxed expression). The path is labeled: 'Automatic, Intuitive, Emotional, Low Effort, Parallel Processing, Error-Prone'. Below are examples: 'Recognizing face', 'Reacting to threat', 'Immediate judgment'. Right side shows 'System 2: Slow Thinking' as a narrow, winding, deliberately-marked pathway with a person concentrating intensely, checking maps, moving slowly. Labeled: 'Deliberate, Logical, Analytical, High Effort, Sequential, Careful'. Examples: 'Complex calculation', 'Careful reasoning', 'Considered decision'. The two pathways sometimes converge and diverge. A central section shows 'Decision Point' where System 1 quickly gives an answer, but System 2 can override with careful analysis. The visual shows: most daily thinking uses System 1 (highway wider and busier), but important decisions should use System 2. A 'Cognitive Load' meter shows System 1 using minimal resources (1%) versus System 2 using substantial resources (30%+).

models.name: Regret Minimization
models.description: A decision-making framework where one projects forward, imagines alternative outcomes, and chooses the path that minimizes potential future regret rather than maximizing immediate gains.
models.when_to_use: When making major life or career decisions, evaluating risk-taking, or dealing with uncertainty
models.visual_description: A timeline extending from Present (center) to Future (right). At the Present point, a decision point shows two paths: 'Safe Path' and 'Bold Path'. The timeline splits into two scenarios. Top scenario follows Safe Path into future showing comfort (relaxed figure), but labeled 'In 10 years: Wondering what if... Regret Level: High'. The person looks back at the Present decision point with regretful expression. Bottom scenario follows Bold Path showing struggle initially (difficult terrain) then success (happy figure celebrating). Labeled 'In 10 years: Achieved goals. Regret Level: Low'. The person looks back with satisfaction. Below each future state, a 'Regret Meter' shows: Safe path = high regret, Bold path = low regret despite difficulty. The framework uses Regret Minimization Framework shown: imagine yourself at age 80 looking back - which decision would you least regret? The visual emphasizes that regret about inaction often exceeds regret about failed action. A mirror shows Present-self making decision, then Future-self evaluating the choice.
